title: “Having gone forth, Anuruddhas, what should a clansman do? Anuruddhas, secluding himself from sensual desires, secluding himself from unwholesome states. If he does not attain…
content: “Having gone forth, Anuruddhas, what should a clansman do? Anuruddhas, secluding himself from sensual desires, secluding himself from unwholesome states. If he does not attain rapture and pleasure or some other state more peaceful than that, then sensual lust invades his mind and remains; ill-will … sloth and torpor … restlessness and remorse … doubt … discontent … laziness invades his mind and remains. Anuruddhas, when he has not secluded himself from sensual desires, from unwholesome states, he does not attain rapture and pleasure or some other state more peaceful than that. Anuruddhas, when he does seclude himself from sensual desires, from unwholesome states, he attains rapture and pleasure or some other state more peaceful than that; then sensual lust does not invade his mind and remain; ill-will … sloth and torpor … restlessness and remorse … doubt … discontent … laziness does not invade his mind and remain. Anuruddhas, when he has secluded himself from sensual desires, from unwholesome states, he attains rapture and pleasure or some other state more peaceful than that.”

title: “If there were no gratification in form, monks, beings would not become lustful for form. But because there is gratification in form, beings become lustful…
content: “If there were no gratification in form, monks, beings would not become lustful for form. But because there is gratification in form, beings become lustful for form. If there were no peril in form, beings would not become disenchanted with form. But because there is peril in form, beings become disenchanted with form. If there were no escape from form, beings would not escape from form. But because there is escape from form, beings escape from form. Monks, as long as beings do not directly know as it actually is the gratification as gratification, the peril as peril, the escape as escape in regard to these six internal sense bases, they have not, monks, along with this world of devas, Māras, Brahmās, with its ascetics and brahmins, with its devas and humans, dwelt with minds free from clinging, detached, liberated, unbounded. But because beings have directly known them as they actually are, therefore they have dwelt with minds free from clinging, detached, liberated, unbounded.”

title: The nun Alavikā, knowing “That is Māra,” recited this verse: There is an escape in the world — I have realized it through wisdom. O…
content:
The nun Alavikā, knowing “That is Māra,” recited this verse:

There is an escape in the world —
I have realized it through wisdom.
O heedless relative (Māra),
How could you know that Dhamma?
Sensual desires are like swords and spears,
The aggregates are a chopping block.
What you call sensual pleasure,
I call no pleasure at all.

title: “There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned. If there were not that unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned, no escape would be discerned from the born,…
content:
“There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned. If there were not that unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned, no escape would be discerned from the born, become, made, conditioned. But because there is an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned, therefore escape is discerned from the born, become, made, conditioned.”

That which is born, become, arisen,
Made, conditioned, impermanent,
Composed of aging and death,
A nest of disease, perishable,
Born from nutriment and craving —
That is not fit to delight in.

But the escape from that —
Peace, beyond reasoning,
Unborn, unbecome,
The sorrowless state, the path without defilement,
The cessation of painful states,
The stilling of conditioned things —
That is happiness.

title: “If he feels a pleasant feeling, he understands that it is impermanent, he understands that it is not to be clung to, he understands that…
content: “If he feels a pleasant feeling, he understands that it is impermanent, he understands that it is not to be clung to, he understands that it is not to be delighted in. If he feels a painful feeling … If he feels a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he understands that it is impermanent, he understands that it is not to be clung to, he understands that it is not to be delighted in. If he feels a pleasant feeling, he feels it with a mind free from attachment. If he feels a painful feeling … If he feels a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he feels it with a mind free from attachment.”

title: “Monks, I have searched for the gratification of form. Whatever gratification there is in form — I have found it. To what extent there is…
content: “Monks, I have searched for the gratification of form. Whatever gratification there is in form — I have found it. To what extent there is gratification in form — that I have seen well with wisdom. Monks, I have searched for the peril of form. Whatever peril there is in form — I have found it. To what extent there is peril in form — that I have seen well with wisdom. Monks, I have searched for the escape from form. Whatever escape there is from form — I have found it. To what extent there is escape from form — that I have seen well with wisdom. Monks, I have searched for the gratification of feeling … of perception … of formations … of consciousness. Whatever gratification there is in consciousness — I have found it. To what extent there is gratification in consciousness — that I have seen well with wisdom. Monks, I have searched for the peril of consciousness … the escape from consciousness … That I have seen well with wisdom. Monks, as long as I did not directly know as it actually is the gratification as gratification, the peril as peril, the escape as escape in regard to these five aggregates of clinging, I did not declare that … ascetics, brahmins, devas, and humans. But when I directly knew them, then the knowledge and vision arose in me: ‘My mind is liberated, imperturbable. This is my last life, now there is no further becoming.’”

title: “Dependent on attachment, suffering arises. All the various sufferings in the world Come into being through attachment. The fool, by making attachments, Again and again…
content:
“Dependent on attachment, suffering arises.
All the various sufferings in the world
Come into being through attachment.
The fool, by making attachments,
Again and again encounters suffering.
Therefore, the wise one, knowing,
Does not create attachments,
Contemplating the nature of birth and suffering.

Those who fall into the stream
Of repeated birth and death,
Going from one existence to another,
Are deluded by ignorance.

This great delusion, ignorance,
Leads to long wandering —
But beings who have attained true knowledge
Do not go to renewed existence.”

title: Elder Nun Sundarī Nandā: Nandā, look at this body — Diseased, impure, stinking. Develop the mind on foulness, One-pointed, well-concentrated. As this body, so that…
content:
Elder Nun Sundarī Nandā:

Nandā, look at this body —
Diseased, impure, stinking.
Develop the mind on foulness,
One-pointed, well-concentrated.

As this body, so that body —
As that body, so this body —
Foul, rotting —
Only fools delight in it.

Thus contemplate this body,
Day and night without fatigue.
With your own wisdom,
Therefore escape.
With your own wisdom,
Escape pride in beauty,
And see truly
What fools see.

This city is built of bones,
Plastered with flesh and blood.
Within dwell aging and death,
Conceit and hypocrisy.

Without heedlessness,
Rightly contemplate that body —
The true nature of this body
Is seen inside and out.
I am disenchanted with the body,
My mind is dispassionate.
Without heedlessness, free from bonds,
I am at peace, stilled.

title: Standing to one side, the deity Tayana recited this verse in the presence of the Blessed One: O brahmin, striving, cut the stream, Abandon and…
content:
Standing to one side, the deity Tayana recited this verse in the presence of the Blessed One:

O brahmin, striving, cut the stream,
Abandon and destroy
All clinging to sensual desires.
Without cutting sensual desires,
A sage cannot attain unification of mind.
If you do what should be done,
Be steadfast and energetic.
If a goer-forth is lazy,
He scatters even more dust.
Not doing is better than doing badly —
Doing badly leads to suffering afterward.
Having done, do it well —
Doing well brings no suffering.
Like holding grass the wrong way,
It can cut your hand,
So too, the ascetic life wrongly practiced
Drags one to hell.
Any lazy conduct,
Any defiled practice,
Any evil action in the holy life
Does not yield great fruit.

title: - “Lust, Venerable Bharadvāja, is the mind. Sometimes, even toward those who are like mothers, lust arises. Toward those like sisters, lust arises. Toward those…
content: - “Lust, Venerable Bharadvāja, is the mind. Sometimes, even toward those who are like mothers, lust arises. Toward those like sisters, lust arises. Toward those like daughters, lust arises. Bharadvāja, is there another cause, another reason why these young monks … live the holy life completely?” - “Great king, the Blessed One, the Knower, the Seer, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One, has said: ‘Come, monks, contemplate this body from the soles of the feet upward, from the top of the head downward, enclosed by skin and full of various impurities. In this body there are: head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, diaphragm, spleen, lungs, intestines, intestinal mesentery, stomach, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, skin oil, saliva, snot, synovial fluid, urine.’ This, great king, is the cause, this is the reason why these young monks live the perfectly pure holy life for their whole life, completing it fully.”
